name: Kamalaśīla
dates_notes: Conventionally dated c. 740–795 CE on the basis of his being Śāntarakṣita's direct disciple (Śāntarakṣita d. 788) and his participation in the famous *Bsam-yas Debate* with the Chinese Chan master Heshang Moheyan, traditionally dated to the 790s. The Tibetan historical literature is the firm anchor; Indian sources are silent.
core_thesis: Kamalaśīla is Śāntarakṣita's principal disciple and the consolidator of the Yogācāra-Mādhyamaka-Svātantrika position. His three *Bhāvanā-Krama* (Stages of Meditation) treatises supply the canonical Mādhyamaka articulation of the gradualist path — meditative analysis (*vipaśyanā*) supported by calm-abiding (*śamatha*), proceeding step by step through the analytic dissolution of svabhāva-construals, culminating in the non-conceptual realization of emptiness. The first *Bhāvanā-Krama* is the principal text and is the Indian Mādhyamaka's most explicit statement that the bodhisattva-path is gradual rather than sudden: there is no shortcut by which non-conceptual realization can be achieved without prior analytic investigation. The position is articulated against the Chan-style claim (which Kamalaśīla encounters historically in the figure of Heshang Moheyan) that the cultivation of non-conceptual awareness without prior analysis is itself the path; Kamalaśīla argues that such cultivation, in the absence of analytic prajñā, cannot dissolve the substantialist habit-energies and so cannot constitute the bodhisattva-path. The *Tattva-Saṅgraha-Pañjikā* (the principal commentary on Śāntarakṣita's *Tattva-Saṅgraha*) supplies the technical apparatus for the polemical chapters, and the *Madhyamakāloka* (Light of the Middle Way) extends the *Madhyamakālaṅkāra*'s analysis with additional articulations of the *neither-one-nor-many* argument and of the relationship between conventional Yogācāra and ultimate Mādhyamaka.

engaged_works.title: Bhavana-Krama I
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Kamalaśīla. The first of three Bhāvanā-Krama treatises. Sanskrit text extant (Tucci 1958 edition, Minor Buddhist Texts II). The three together survive in Tibetan translation (Toh. 3915–3917).

ascription_evidence.description: Tucci 1958 critical edition; Sharma 1997 study and translation.

engaged_works.summary: A prose treatise on the cultivation of the bodhisattva-path articulated in terms of the integration of *śamatha* (calm-abiding) and *vipaśyanā* (analytic insight). The treatise establishes that the bodhisattva-path requires both: śamatha without vipaśyanā produces stabilization without liberation, and vipaśyanā without śamatha produces conceptual understanding without transformative realization. The analytic content of vipaśyanā is the Mādhyamaka analysis of emptiness — the *neither-one-nor-many* argument and its corollaries — and the meditative result is the non-conceptual realization of emptiness that the analytic investigation prepares. The position is articulated against the suddenist claim that non-conceptual awareness can be cultivated directly. The treatise is the principal Indian Mādhyamaka source for the gradualist account of the path and is the doctrinal seed of Tsong-kha-pa's *Lam Rim* tradition in Tibet.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Sangraha-Panjika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Kamalaśīla as the principal commentary on his teacher Śāntarakṣita's Tattva-Saṅgraha. The Pañjikā is transmitted with the root-text in the standard editions (Krishnamacharya 1926, Shastri 1968).
engaged_works.summary: A complete prose commentary on Śāntarakṣita's twenty-six-chapter doxographical-polemical *Tattva-Saṅgraha*. The Pañjikā supplies the exegetical apparatus that makes the root-text's compressed verse-arguments usable: identifying the opponent's exact position, citing the relevant primary sources, articulating the precise logical structure of the refutation, and drawing out the implications for the constructive Yogācāra-Mādhyamaka-Svātantrika position. Without the Pañjikā the *Tattva-Saṅgraha* is largely unreadable; with it the pair forms the most thorough doxographical resource on eighth-century Indian inter-darśana debate available from any source.

engaged_works.title: Madhyamakaloka
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Kamalaśīla. Sanskrit lost; Tibetan translation Toh. 3887. Studied by Keira (2004) and others.
engaged_works.summary: An extended prose treatise developing the Madhyamaka analysis of emptiness with particular attention to the relationship between conventional Yogācāra and ultimate Madhyamaka — the structural premise of the school. The treatise refines the *neither-one-nor-many* argument with additional case-studies and engages systematically with the alternative Madhyamaka positions of the period (Bhāviveka, Candrakīrti, and the Buddhapālita lineage). The text is the most extended Indian articulation of the Yogācāra-Madhyamaka-Svātantrika synthesis and is the principal philosophical resource for the later Tibetan elaboration of the position.

reception_notes: Kamalaśīla is the principal channel through which the Yogācāra-Madhyamaka-Svātantrika position enters Tibet, and the *Bsam-yas Debate* — in which the Indian gradualist position is traditionally held to have prevailed over the Chinese suddenist position — is the foundational event of Tibetan Buddhist doctrinal identity. The historical reality of the debate is contested by modern scholarship (Demiéville 1952, Le Concile de Lhasa; Gomez 1983), but its doctrinal significance for the Tibetan tradition is uncontested. Sanskrit Vedāntic engagement with Kamalaśīla is indirect and proceeds via the Mādhyamaka heading.
